content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 16, 2016

Joseph M. Montes
Partner
Burke Williams & Sorensen, LLP
444 South Flower Street, Suite 2400
Los Angeles, CA 90071-2953

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-16-112

Dear Mr. Montes:

       This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Clarita Councilmembers
Laurene Weste and Timben Boydston regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

       Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

       May Councilmember Weste and Councilmember Boydston participate in (1) a possible
upcoming Council decision limiting retail uses in the Urban Center Zone to those related to arts and
entertainment or (2) a decision requiring businesses to provide parking or pay in-lieu parking fees to
the City to subsidize its building parking structures in connection with development within the
Specific Plan area?

CONCLUSION

       No. Councilmember Weste and Councilmember Boydston may not participate in any
decision limiting retail uses in the Urban Center Zone or any decision requiring parking or payment
of in-lieu of parking fees in connection with development within the Specific Plan area because
there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their interests.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-112
Page No. 2

FACTS

Your office serves as the City Attorney for the City of Santa Clarita and you are writing on
behalf of Councilmembers Laurene Weste and Timben Boydston regarding the Old Town Newhall
Specific Plan, as well as consideration of new parking regulations.

The Old Town Newhall Specific Plan was adopted by the City Council in 2005 and outlined
the vision for the future of the Old Town Newhall. Since the adoption of the specific plan, the City
of Santa Clarita and the former Redevelopment Agency of the City of Santa Clarita have invested
considerable resources in Old Town Newhall, with most investment centered on Main Street in the
Urban Center zone. These resources were committed in an effort to revitalize Newhall's historic
core and create a district filled with restaurants and arts and entertainment venues. As the area has
revitalized, the Main Street area has become more attractive to all types of uses, including retail
uses that do not support the arts and entertainment vision. Additionally, concerns about parking
requirements as set forth in the Specific Plan have also been raised.

The City Council may be considering an ordinance which would limit the types of retail
uses within the Urban Center Zone of the Specific Plan to only those uses that are specifically
related to arts and entertainment.

In terms of parking, the Specific Plan does not require parking for commercial and retail
development. Instead, the Specific Plan contemplated that the Santa Clarita Redevelopment Agency
would construct two parking structures (one at each end of the Urban Center Zone) that would
provide parking for development in the Specific Plan Area. With the dissolution of redevelopment
agencies by the State Legislature, the City Council may be considering an ordinance that would
require retail and commercial development in the Specific Plan to either provide on-site parking in
connection with development, or require payment of an in-lieu fee to subsidize the construction of
parking by the City.

Councilmember Weste owns property within 500 feet of the two northeastern most blocks of
the Urban Center Zone of the Specific Plan. This property is approximately seven acres and is a
horse ranch on which she has lived for over forty years. The property is separated from the Urban
Center Zone of the Specific Plan's boundaries by Railroad Avenue, the Union Pacific Railroad right
of way, and there is a hill between the Urban Center Zone of the Specific Plan and her residence
that currently block views of the Urban Center Zone of the Specific Plan from her residence.

Current access between the Urban Center Zone of the Specific Plan and her property is
circuitous, requiring travel north on Railroad Avenue to 13th Street, crossing the railroad tracks at
that location, and then proceeding on Arch Street to Placerita Canyon Road to her property.
However, the City's General Plan Circulation element contemplates more direct access via an
extension of Lyons Avenue across the railroad tracks through or near Councilmember Weste's
property. The City is currently undertaking environmental and alignment studies for the roadway
extension and the timing of its construction is not yet known.

Councilmember Boydston serves as the salaried Executive Director of the Canyon Theatre
Guild (CTG). CTG is a nonprofit theater company that owns and operates the Canyon Theater.

File No. A-16-112
Page No. 3

CTG puts on several live theater productions per year. The theater is located at 24242 Main Street, which is located within the Urban Center Zone of the Specific Plan. At this time, the theater does not have any plans to convert its use to retail or sell its property for development.

You would like to know whether Councilmember Weste and Councilmember Boydston may participate in any decision limiting retail uses in the Urban Center Zone or any decision requiring parking or payment in-lieu of parking fees in connection with development within the Specific Plan area.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the interests specified in Section 87103. Councilmembers Weste and Boydston have interests specified in Section 87103 as follows.

Councilmember Weste

Councilmember Weste has a financial interest in residential real property within 500 feet of the Urban Zone. This residence is not "explicitly involved" in the decisions regarding the uses in the Urban Zone or the parking. (See Regulation 18701(a).) When the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) If a financial effect is not reasonably foreseeable, then there is no conflict under the Act. (Regulation 18700(d)(1).) Under Regulation 18701(b), the factors to consider when a financial interest is not explicitly involved to determine foreseeability include:

"(1) The extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent upon intervening events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency.

"(2) Whether the public official should anticipate a financial effect on his or her financial interest as a potential outcome under normal circumstances when using appropriate due diligence and care.

"(3) Whether the public official has a financial interest that is of the type that would typically be affected by the terms of the governmental decision or whether the governmental decision is of the type that would be expected to have a financial effect on businesses and individuals similarly situated to those businesses and individuals in which the public official has a financial interest.

"(4) Whether a reasonable inference can be made that the financial effects of the governmental decision on the public official's financial interest might

File No. A-16-112
Page No. 4

compromise a public official's ability to act in a manner consistent with his or her
duty to act in the best interests of the public.

"(5) Whether the governmental decision will provide or deny an opportunity,
or create an advantage or disadvantage for one of the official's financial interests,
including whether the financial interest may be entitled to compete or be eligible for
a benefit resulting from the decision.

"(6) Whether the public official has the type of financial interest that would
cause a similarly situated person to weigh the advantages and disadvantages of the
governmental decision on his or her financial interest in formulating a position."

While Councilmember Weste's property is geographically separated by a hill that blocks the
view of the Urban Center Zone and the current route to property from the Urban Center Zone is
circuitous, you have stated that more direct access to the property from the Urban Center Zone is
being studied. In this case, considering that Councilmember Weste's property is very close to the
future development and that more direct access to her property is contemplated, it is reasonably
foreseeable that under normal circumstances there would be a financial effect upon her real
property as a result of limiting the uses in the Old Town Newhall area and as a result of proposed
parking regulations.

Because there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect upon the real property, we next
consider whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is material. (Regulation 18700(d)(3).)

Regulation 18702.2 provides the relevant standards for determining when a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect is material. The criteria in Regulation 18702.2 most relevant to
Councilmember Weste's real property are whether the governmental decision involves the
following:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial
interest;

"(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1."

Considering that the Old Town Newhall Urban Center Zone redevelopment project is
located within 500 feet of Councilmember Weste's real property and may affect the value of
Councilmember Weste's residential property for several reasons including, but not limited to
changing the development potential of the large tract bordering the city's Urban Center, traffic
levels, privacy, noise levels, and parking we find sufficient facts to indicate that there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Councilmember Weste's financial interest.

File No. A-16-112
Page No. 5

Councilmember Weste is therefore prohibited from making, participating in making, or using her
position to influence any decisions regarding limiting retail uses in the Urban Center Zone and
parking or payment of in-lieu parking fees.

Councilmember Boydston

Councilmember Boydston has a financial interest in his source of income (salary), received
from a non-profit business entity, CTG. (See Section 87103(c) and Regulation 18700.1(a).) CTG
and its property (Canyon Theatre) is "explicitly involved" in the decision regarding the Urban Zone.
(Regulation 18701(a).) It is located in the Urban Zone, and is the subject of the decisions regarding
limiting uses of property in the Urban Center Zone and parking/payment in-lieu parking fees.
Therefore, it is presumed that there will be a reasonably foreseeable financial effect upon CTG.
(Regulation 18701 (a).) The issue that remains is whether the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of decision would be material on CTG. For a source of income, Regulation 18702. 3 provides
the following standards:

"(a) . . . For income received by the official . . . including salary, the
financial effect is material if:

"(1) The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is
otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding; or

"(3) The source is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial
benefit or loss, or the official knows or has reason to know that the nonprofit has
an interest in real property that will be financially affected under the standards
applied to a financial interest in Regulation 18702.2."

Regulation 18702.2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision is
material whenever the governmental decision in involves certain factors that affect the real property
in question including:

"(1) The adoption of or amendment to a general (except as provided
below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries
of the plan..."

In this case, CTG is the subject of the decision before Councilmember Boydston. Moreover,
the decision involves an amendment to a specific plan. Accordingly, there will be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon Councilmember Boydston's interest in CTG under both
Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) and (3) and he is prohibited from participating in the decisions regarding
limiting retail uses in the Urban Center Zone and parking or payment of in-lieu parking fees.

File No. A-16-112
Page No. 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Sukhi K. Brar
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

upcoming Council decision limiting retail uses in the Urban Center Zone to those related to arts and 
entertainment or (2) a decision requiring businesses to provide parking or pay in-lieu parking fees to 
the City to subsidize its building parking structures in connection with development within the 
Specific Plan area?

CONCLUSION: No. Councilmember Weste and Councilmember Boydston may not participate in any 
decision limiting retail uses in the Urban Center Zone or any decision requiring parking or payment 
of in-lieu of parking fees in connection with development within the Specific Plan area because 
there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their interests.All 
regulatory referdnces are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code ofRegulatioos. unless otherwise indicated.
